Issue.title: Issue
Issue.description: Represents a reported maintenance or IT issue.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Issue entity.

category.description: The main category of the issue (Facility, IT, Purchase).

subCategory.description: The sub-category of the issue (e.g., external, internal for Facility; hardware, software for IT).

status.description: The current status of the issue (accepted, pending, ongoing, finished).

assignedTo.description: The role or user to whom the issue is assigned (Operations Manager, IT Manager, Purchaser, Admin Director).

reportedBy.description: Reference to User. (Relationship: User 1:N Issue). The user who reported the issue.

dateReported.description: The date and time when the issue was reported.

User.title: User
User.description: Represents a user of the application.

id.description: Unique identifier for the User entity.

email.description: The email address of the user.

role.description: The role of the user (Operations Manager, IT Manager, Purchaser, Admin Director).

definition.description: Stores user profiles. The `userId` parameter is the unique identifier for each user.
Stores issues reported by each user. Includes denormalized 'reportedBy' field for authorization independence.

params.name: userId
params.description: Unique identifier for the user who reported the issue.

firestore.reasoning:
The Firestore data structure is designed to support the TrackWise application's features, focusing on issue reporting, role-based routing, and status tracking. It leverages path-based ownership and denormalization to ensure authorization independence and efficient querying. Separate collections are used to manage users and issues. User roles are directly stored in the user document. Issues are stored in a collection under the user's document, enabling easy querying of issues reported by a specific user. 

Authorization Independence is achieved by storing all necessary authorization data (the `reportedBy` field, which contains the user ID) within the issue document itself. This eliminates the need for security rules to perform `get()` operations on parent documents, which ensures that issue creation and modification can be performed atomically without relying on hierarchical authorization dependencies. This denormalization strategy is crucial for maintaining data integrity and simplifying security rules.

The structure also supports QAPs (Rules are not Filters) by ensuring that listing issues for a user does not require filtering based on issue content. The path-based structure `/users/{userId}/issues/{issueId}` allows direct access and listing of issues associated with a specific user, while security rules can efficiently control access based on the `{userId}` parameter without inspecting the contents of each issue document. Segregation is achieved because all documents in `/users/{userId}/issues` share the same security posture, i.e. only the user with id `{userId}` can access them.
